meta.title: How Much Does a Website Cost? A Honest Breakdown for Small Businesses
meta.description: Real pricing, hidden fees, and what ownership actually means in 2026. By Alex, web developer in Rome.

title: How Much Does a Website Cost?
lead: A small business website typically costs €500-€5,000. Most land in the €1,000-€2,500 range. Here is what that gap actually means.

sections.heading: Why website prices vary so wildly
paras: The difference is who builds it and how.
A €150 quote means a pre-built template on a platform you don't own. It won't rank on Google, won't load fast on mobile, and disappears the moment you stop paying.
A €10,000 agency quote covers project managers, account managers, and brand workshops. You are paying for their overhead, not your results.
A freelance developer builds from scratch - no template, no recurring fees to them. For most small businesses, that is exactly what they need.

sections.heading: What you are actually paying for
paras: Design: how it guides a visitor toward contacting you - every button, headline, and colour is a conversion decision.
Code quality: sub-2-second load time is not an accident. It requires clean code, optimised assets, caching, and correct hosting. Google measures Core Web Vitals. Slow sites rank lower.
SEO foundation: title tags, meta descriptions, Schema Markup, XML sitemap, Google Search Console setup. Without these, you are invisible.
Mobile: 70%+ of small business visitors arrive on a phone. A poor mobile layout loses them in under 3 seconds.
Ownership: you own the result - or you don't. Most people only regret this later.

sections.heading: The hidden costs nobody mentions upfront
paras: Domain: €10-15/year - you pay this regardless of who builds your site.
Hosting: €5-25/month. Some developers mark it up. Others build on Vercel where basic hosting is free.
CMS subscriptions: Squarespace or Wix cost €13-40/month forever. Stop paying, site goes down.
Update charges: some agencies charge €50-150 to change a phone number. Ask before signing: who makes changes, and at what cost?
Plugin renewals: WordPress sites often depend on paid plugins for SEO, bookings, and forms - each renewing annually. A €800 build can accumulate €300/year in plugin fees within two years.

sections.heading: What owning the code actually means
paras: At launch you receive everything: source code, domain, hosting access, design files. You are not renting from me.
You can move hosts, hire any developer for changes, and the site keeps running if I disappeared tomorrow. No monthly fee.
This is not industry-standard. Ask explicitly before you commit to any developer.

sections.heading: When does it cost more?
paras: Booking + payment systems: integrating a payment processor, calendar logic, and automated confirmations adds significant build time.
Multilingual sites: three language versions mean three sets of every page plus a language-detection layer.
Custom animations: time-intensive to do correctly.
E-commerce: a product catalogue, cart, and checkout is a different scale of project entirely.
Tight deadlines: a 5-day turnaround costs more than a 2-week timeline.
If none of these apply, a professional 5-page business site should not exceed €2,000.

costTable.heading: What does a website cost to run after launch?
costTable.intro: For a custom site on modern hosting infrastructure:
rows: Per hour, only when you ask
costTable.note: Most small business sites I build cost under €15/year to keep running after launch.

items.q: Can I get a website for under 500 euros?
items.a: Yes - but it will be a template on a platform you pay monthly for. Fine as a placeholder. It will not generate leads.

items.q: Do I need to pay monthly for a website?
items.a: Not for a custom-built site on modern infrastructure. Platform sites like Squarespace and Wix always require a monthly subscription. Ask your developer upfront.

items.q: How long does it take to build a website?
items.a: 1-2 weeks from content-ready to launch for a standard site. Delays almost always come from waiting on copy, photos, and feedback - not development.

items.q: Is a cheap website ever the right choice?
items.a: For a brand-new business that needs something live immediately, yes. A €200 Squarespace site beats nothing. Once you have clients, invest in a proper build.

items.q: What is the difference between a freelancer and an agency?
items.a: Overhead. An agency charges you for project managers, designers, developers, and account teams. A freelancer is the person who actually builds the site - same result, fraction of the cost.

items.q: Will my website show up on Google?
items.a: A properly built site with SEO foundations gets indexed within days of launch. Ranking for competitive terms takes months of real traffic data. Anyone guaranteeing the top position is lying.

items.q: Do I need a blog to rank on Google?
items.a: Not for local searches or your business name. A blog helps only if you publish content your clients are actively searching. Three well-written guide articles outperform a year of generic weekly posts.

items.q: How do I know if the price is fair?
items.a: Ask for an itemised quote: design, development, SEO setup, and what is included post-launch. A developer who works transparently gives you this without hesitation. Vague pricing signals vague work.

authorBio: Written by Alex, web developer based in Rome, Italy. I build custom websites for small businesses across Italy and Europe.
authorCta: Get a free quote on WhatsApp
